Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'm juggling a lot with limited resources, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could find a quiet moment. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often print materials or sketch out concepts when I had access to a printer, then study from those notes later. I aimed for about 11 hours a week, but it was rarely at consistent times. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, but honestly, when assignments were due or I had competing responsibilities, everything would shift. I'd cram more studying into certain days and barely touch anything on others. Being 25 and managing life stuff alongside 16 online courses meant I couldn't always stick to an ideal schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—like an upcoming deadline or to watch a lecture video I'd been putting off. Sometimes the gamified tasks would prompt me to log in because they felt less overwhelming than regular work. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse; I needed a concrete reason because my time and energy were limited.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, but then life would get hectic and I'd fall out of that rhythm. The gamified elements helped a bit—they made logging in feel less like a chore—but I never reached a point where it was automatic. My engagement level stayed pretty low overall.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I'd watch them carefully, sometimes pausing to draw diagrams or take visual notes. Anything with clear graphics or demonstrations really stuck with me. I spent a lot of time on videos—probably around three hours total—because that format just clicks with my brain better than reading dense text.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. With my anxiety being what it is, posting publicly felt really intimidating, so I only posted a handful of times. I also delayed a lot of the smaller tasks and assignments—my completion rate on those was pretty low. It's not that I didn't think they were valuable; I just had to prioritize what would impact my grade most directly given my limited time and energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exams or if it helped me visualize a concept I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were interesting because they broke things into smaller, less intimidating pieces. But honestly, with high anxiety and medium stress, I had to be ruthless about where I spent my energy. If something felt like busywork, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones created pressure, which my anxious brain both hated and needed to actually get moving. Optional stuff would sit in my mental to-do list, adding to my stress, but I rarely got to them. The gamified tasks were a nice middle ground—they felt optional but engaging enough that I'd sometimes do them when I needed a break from heavier material.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study from them offline for days. I made tons of handwritten visual notes, diagrams, and mind maps that I'd review constantly. I also used library textbooks since I couldn't afford to buy my own. Most of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just with materials I'd gathered from it.
Q10: Most of my study time wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours working through problems on paper, creating color-coded diagrams, or reviewing printed materials on the bus. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person sometimes, working through concepts together. The LMS would just show me downloading something once, but I'd use that material for days afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then that would be my study material for the week. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned early on to grab everything I needed in one session. The LMS would show minimal activity, but I was genuinely engaging with those materials deeply—just offline where nobody could track it.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, usually when my anxiety spiked or when multiple deadlines collided across my 16 courses. Mid-semester was brutal. I'd feel paralyzed sometimes, knowing I had so much to do but feeling unable to start. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming. My attendance dropped during those periods, and I'd fall behind on assignments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS became something I avoided because it reminded me of everything I wasn't doing. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, family things—would eat into my study time. Confusion about course material would make me anxious, and then I'd procrastinate on engaging with it. It became a cycle where the stress made me avoid the very thing causing the stress.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions—technically I was 'studying,' but nothing was sticking. My anxiety made it hard to be present sometimes.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though I knew they mattered. My assignment completion was really low despite caring about the course. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt harder than continuing. Part of it was poor time management across too many courses. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would bring new urgencies. I'm not proud of it, but that's honestly what happened.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage sometimes. I'd have this burst of activity right before something was due. The LMS probably shows my logins clustering around deadline dates. It's not healthy, but the pressure of a deadline would override my anxiety enough to actually get me moving. Without deadlines, I'd drift.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more. But waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, and critical feedback could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a bit. Overall, I think feedback helped, but my emotional response to it was intense. I performed well on exams, so positive feedback there kept me going.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. When things were clearly laid out with visual schedules and explicit deadlines, I could manage better. The gamified elements helped break up the monotony and gave me smaller wins. But with 16 courses, even well-structured ones became overwhelming. I appreciated clear organization because my brain was already juggling so much.
Q19: Visual materials and clear navigation made engagement easier. Anything that required extensive reading or wasn't well-organized made it harder—my anxiety would spike when I couldn't figure out where to find things. The gamified tasks were actually helpful because they felt achievable. Discussion requirements were harder for me because of social anxiety. Flexibility in deadlines would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's anxious and overwhelmed. You might log in repeatedly, watch videos, read materials, but then freeze when it comes to actually submitting work. The submission part requires a different kind of energy—putting yourself out there for evaluation. Someone could be genuinely learning but struggling with the final step of turning something in.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. I got a Distinction and scored nearly 90 on exams, but my LMS engagement was low and my task completion was around 30%. I studied effectively offline, focused intensely on exam preparation, and absorbed material through visual methods that didn't require constant LMS activity. The system can't see that I was reviewing printed notes on the bus every day.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long videos play. But they miss everything that happens offline, which for me was most of my actual learning. They can't measure understanding or the quality of engagement. They might show I watched a video, but not that I paused it fifty times to draw diagrams. They miss the human complexity.
Q23: For me, being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, when concepts are clicking, when I'm making connections between ideas. It's not about how often I log in—it's about whether my brain is actually working with the content. Some of my most engaged moments happened with a notebook and printed slides, nowhere near a computer.
Q24: So much. My anxiety levels, my resilience in pushing through despite feeling overwhelmed, the hours I spent with handwritten notes and diagrams. The fact that I was managing 16 courses with low resources and still achieved a Distinction. The emotional labor of showing up when everything felt hard. The LMS sees a disengaged student with low activity; it doesn't see someone fighting anxiety every day to learn.
